item.title: Structure-Guided Domain-Adaptive Network for Few-Shot SAR Ship Detection
item.dc:creator: Kang Ni
Weihang Zhou
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Neural Networks and Learning Systems
item.description: Due to the complexity of synthetic aperture radar (SAR) imaging mechanisms, SAR ship detection faces challenges such as difficulty in sample annotation and the influence of complex backgrounds, leading to poor target readability and difficulties in feature representation. Compared to SAR images, optical remote sensing images offer advantages such as high resolution and intuitive visualization, making them complementary to SAR images. Based on this, this article leverages the more intuitive structural features of optical remote sensing images to guide SAR ship target feature learning and proposes the structure-guided domain-adaptive network (SGDANet) for few-shot SAR ship detection. This proposed network follows a convolutional neural networks (CNNs)-transformer architecture, modeling the structural and edge token features of optical remote sensing images and embedding them into the network. Additionally, a feature fusion mechanism based on the split-fuse-merge strategy and attention mechanism is designed to achieve improved domain adaptation performance during the adversarial learning stage. Experiments on three self-built ship datasets illustrate that SGDANet outperforms other related network models in both three-shot and five-shot scenarios. Notably, SGDANet also exhibits good performance in zero-shot SAR target detection tasks, indicating its strong generalization ability. Code is available at https://github.com/RSIP-NJUPT/SGDANet Published: 2026-06-08T19:52:28+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Neural Networks and Learning Systems Score: 0.862 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Kang Ni; Weihang Zhou

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Neural Networks and Learning Systems

DOI: 10.1109/tnnls.2026.3699093

Relevance: 0.862 (must_read)

Due to the complexity of synthetic aperture radar (SAR) imaging mechanisms, SAR ship detection faces challenges such as difficulty in sample annotation and the influence of complex backgrounds, leading to poor target readability and difficulties in feature representation. Compared to SAR images, optical remote sensing images offer advantages such as high resolution and intuitive visualization, making them complementary to SAR images. Based on this, this article leverages the more intuitive structural features of optical remote sensing images to guide SAR ship target feature learning and proposes the structure-guided domain-adaptive network (SGDANet) for few-shot SAR ship detection. This proposed network follows a convolutional neural networks (CNNs)-transformer architecture, modeling the structural and edge token features of optical remote sensing images and embedding them into the network. Additionally, a feature fusion mechanism based on the split-fuse-merge strategy and attention mechanism is designed to achieve improved domain adaptation performance during the adversarial learning stage. Experiments on three self-built ship datasets illustrate that SGDANet outperforms other related network models in both three-shot and five-shot scenarios. Notably, SGDANet also exhibits good performance in zero-shot SAR target detection tasks, indicating its strong generalization ability. Code is available at https://github.com/RSIP-NJUPT/SGDANet

item.title: Transformers with Joint Tokens and Local-Global Attention for Efficient Human Pose Estimation
item.dc:creator: Kaleab A. Kinfu
René Vidal
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Pattern Analysis and Machine Intelligence
item.description: Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) and Vision Transformers (ViTs) have led to significant progress in 2D body pose estimation. However, achieving a good balance between accuracy, efficiency, and robustness remains a challenge. For instance, CNNs are computationally efficient but struggle with long-range dependencies, while ViTs excel in capturing such dependencies but suffer from quadratic computational complexity. This paper proposes two ViT-based models for accurate, efficient, and robust 2D pose estimation. The first one, EViTPose, operates in a computationally efficient manner without sacrificing accuracy by utilizing learnable joint tokens to select and process a subset of the most important body patches, enabling us to control the trade-off between accuracy and efficiency by changing the number of patches to be processed. The second one, UniTransPose, while not allowing for the same level of direct control over the trade-off, efficiently handles multiple scales by combining (1) an efficient multi-scale transformer encoder that uses both local and global attention with (2) an efficient sub-pixel CNN decoder for better speed and accuracy. Moreover, by incorporating all joints from different benchmarks into a unified skeletal representation, we train robust methods that learn from multiple datasets simultaneously and perform well across a range of scenarios, including pose variations, lighting conditions, and occlusions. Experiments on six benchmarks demonstrate that the proposed methods significantly outperform state-of-the-art methods while improving computational efficiency. EViTPose exhibits a significant decrease in computational complexity (30% to 44% less in GFLOPs) with a minimal drop of accuracy (0% to 3.5% less), and UniTransPose achieves accuracy improvements ranging from 0.9% to 43.8% across these benchmarks. Published: 2026-06-08T19:51:25+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Pattern Analysis and Machine Intelligence Score: 0.841 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Kaleab A. Kinfu; René Vidal

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Pattern Analysis and Machine Intelligence

DOI: 10.1109/tpami.2026.3701408

Relevance: 0.841 (must_read)

Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) and Vision Transformers (ViTs) have led to significant progress in 2D body pose estimation. However, achieving a good balance between accuracy, efficiency, and robustness remains a challenge. For instance, CNNs are computationally efficient but struggle with long-range dependencies, while ViTs excel in capturing such dependencies but suffer from quadratic computational complexity. This paper proposes two ViT-based models for accurate, efficient, and robust 2D pose estimation. The first one, EViTPose, operates in a computationally efficient manner without sacrificing accuracy by utilizing learnable joint tokens to select and process a subset of the most important body patches, enabling us to control the trade-off between accuracy and efficiency by changing the number of patches to be processed. The second one, UniTransPose, while not allowing for the same level of direct control over the trade-off, efficiently handles multiple scales by combining (1) an efficient multi-scale transformer encoder that uses both local and global attention with (2) an efficient sub-pixel CNN decoder for better speed and accuracy. Moreover, by incorporating all joints from different benchmarks into a unified skeletal representation, we train robust methods that learn from multiple datasets simultaneously and perform well across a range of scenarios, including pose variations, lighting conditions, and occlusions. Experiments on six benchmarks demonstrate that the proposed methods significantly outperform state-of-the-art methods while improving computational efficiency. EViTPose exhibits a significant decrease in computational complexity (30% to 44% less in GFLOPs) with a minimal drop of accuracy (0% to 3.5% less), and UniTransPose achieves accuracy improvements ranging from 0.9% to 43.8% across these benchmarks.

item.title: C-DiffSET: Leveraging Latent Diffusion for SAR-to-EO Image Translation with Confidence-Guided Reliable Object Generation
item.dc:creator: Jeonghyeok Do
Jaehyup Lee
Seungchul Lee
Munchurl Kim
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology
item.description: Synthetic Aperture Radar (SAR) imagery provides robust environmental and temporal coverage (e.g., during clouds, seasons, day-night cycles), yet its noise and unique structural patterns pose interpretation challenges, especially for non-experts. SAR-to-EO (Electro-Optical) image translation (SET) has emerged to make SAR images more perceptually interpretable. However, traditional approaches, trained from scratch on limited SAR-EO datasets, are prone to overfitting and struggle with dataset inconsistencies. To address these challenges, we introduce Confidence Diffusion for SAR-to-EO Translation (C-DiffSET), a framework leveraging a pretrained Latent Diffusion Model (LDM) to effectively adapt its extensive generative priors from natural images to the EO domain. Our investigation reveals that the pretrained VAE encoder effectively aligns SAR and EO images within a shared latent space, demonstrating robustness even to varying noise levels in SAR inputs. To further improve pixel-wise fidelity for SET and mitigate artifacts from temporal discrepancies, such as appearing or disappearing objects, we propose a novel confidence-guided diffusion (C-Diff) loss. This loss dynamically guides the diffusion process to down-weight penalties in uncertain regions, thereby enhancing structural accuracy. C-DiffSET achieves state-of-the-art (SOTA) results on multiple benchmark datasets (QXS-SAROPT, SAR2Opt, SpaceNet6, Stellar-Vision), significantly outperforming recent image-to-image translation methods and specialized SET methods across all standard metrics. The source code and trained models are available at https://github.com/KAIST-VICLab/C-DiffSET. Published: 2026-06-09T19:55:00+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology Score: 0.832 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Jeonghyeok Do; Jaehyup Lee; Seungchul Lee; Munchurl Kim

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology

DOI: 10.1109/tcsvt.2026.3701447

Relevance: 0.832 (must_read)

Synthetic Aperture Radar (SAR) imagery provides robust environmental and temporal coverage (e.g., during clouds, seasons, day-night cycles), yet its noise and unique structural patterns pose interpretation challenges, especially for non-experts. SAR-to-EO (Electro-Optical) image translation (SET) has emerged to make SAR images more perceptually interpretable. However, traditional approaches, trained from scratch on limited SAR-EO datasets, are prone to overfitting and struggle with dataset inconsistencies. To address these challenges, we introduce Confidence Diffusion for SAR-to-EO Translation (C-DiffSET), a framework leveraging a pretrained Latent Diffusion Model (LDM) to effectively adapt its extensive generative priors from natural images to the EO domain. Our investigation reveals that the pretrained VAE encoder effectively aligns SAR and EO images within a shared latent space, demonstrating robustness even to varying noise levels in SAR inputs. To further improve pixel-wise fidelity for SET and mitigate artifacts from temporal discrepancies, such as appearing or disappearing objects, we propose a novel confidence-guided diffusion (C-Diff) loss. This loss dynamically guides the diffusion process to down-weight penalties in uncertain regions, thereby enhancing structural accuracy. C-DiffSET achieves state-of-the-art (SOTA) results on multiple benchmark datasets (QXS-SAROPT, SAR2Opt, SpaceNet6, Stellar-Vision), significantly outperforming recent image-to-image translation methods and specialized SET methods across all standard metrics. The source code and trained models are available at https://github.com/KAIST-VICLab/C-DiffSET.

item.title: Physically consistent multitemporal dual-domain learning for dual-polarization SAR despeckling
item.dc:creator: Jiangong Xu
Yang Yang
Weibao Xue
Xiaoyu Yu
Junli Li
Jun Pan
Mi Wang
item.prism:publicationName: International Journal of Applied Earth Observation and Geoinformation
item.description: Speckle contamination strongly affects the interpretability and practical application of synthetic aperture radar (SAR) imagery. For real dual-polarization SAR data, despeckling is particularly difficult because clean supervision is unavailable and temporal variations between acquisitions complicate the characterization of speckle behavior. Existing unsupervised deep models have shown promise, but many of them still underuse the complementary information contained in neighboring temporal observations and insufficiently exploit the physical cues associated with polarimetric scattering, which may result in structural degradation and distortion of scattering characteristics. To address these limitations, we develop CD 2 P-Net, a collaborative dual-domain progressive network for real dual-polarization SAR despeckling. The method is established within a physically guided multitemporal unsupervised learning framework, where spatial, spectral, and temporal relationships are progressively modeled to make better use of temporal coherence and speckle variability across adjacent acquisitions. In addition, covariance-derived statistics and polarimetric decomposition descriptors are introduced to improve the physical reliability of the learned features. Experiments on real Sentinel-1 dual-polarization time-series data show that the proposed method achieves superior despeckling performance compared with representative competing approaches, while better preserving spatial structures, polarimetric characteristics, and semantic consistency. Published: 2026-06-08T23:41:46+00:00 Venue: International Journal of Applied Earth Observation and Geoinformation Score: 0.817 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Jiangong Xu; Yang Yang; Weibao Xue; Xiaoyu Yu; Junli Li; Jun Pan; Mi Wang

Journal: International Journal of Applied Earth Observation and Geoinformation

DOI: 10.1016/j.jag.2026.105409

Relevance: 0.817 (must_read)

Speckle contamination strongly affects the interpretability and practical application of synthetic aperture radar (SAR) imagery. For real dual-polarization SAR data, despeckling is particularly difficult because clean supervision is unavailable and temporal variations between acquisitions complicate the characterization of speckle behavior. Existing unsupervised deep models have shown promise, but many of them still underuse the complementary information contained in neighboring temporal observations and insufficiently exploit the physical cues associated with polarimetric scattering, which may result in structural degradation and distortion of scattering characteristics. To address these limitations, we develop CD 2 P-Net, a collaborative dual-domain progressive network for real dual-polarization SAR despeckling. The method is established within a physically guided multitemporal unsupervised learning framework, where spatial, spectral, and temporal relationships are progressively modeled to make better use of temporal coherence and speckle variability across adjacent acquisitions. In addition, covariance-derived statistics and polarimetric decomposition descriptors are introduced to improve the physical reliability of the learned features. Experiments on real Sentinel-1 dual-polarization time-series data show that the proposed method achieves superior despeckling performance compared with representative competing approaches, while better preserving spatial structures, polarimetric characteristics, and semantic consistency.

item.title: STGBD-Net: Spatio-temporal Gradient Basis Decomposition Network for Infrared Small Target Detection
item.dc:creator: Chen Hu
Mingyu Zhou
Shuai Yuan
Hongbo Hu
Zhenming Peng
Tian Pu
Xiying Li
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
item.description: A key challenge in infrared small target detection (IRSTD) is that weak target signal responses are easily obscured by strong background clutter, frequently resulting in missed detections. While traditional gradient-based methods attempt to capture fine details, their robustness is limited by the static fusion of multi-directional gradient features. In this paper, we rethink feature fusion from the perspective of Basis Decomposition Theory and propose a novel framework that reformulates the process into an explicit and adaptive decomposition-and-reconstruction paradigm. Specifically, we introduce the Basis Decomposition Module (BDM) and its specialized variant, the Gradient Decomposition Module (GDM) for IRSTD. GDMs treat the normalized gradient features as basis vectors to reconstruct a new feature, thereby maintaining detailed structures and highlighting infrared small targets. By integrating GDMs into a lightweight three-stage U-Net, we develop two unified architectures: the Spatial Gradient Basis Decomposition Network for single-frame detection and the Spatio-temporal Gradient Basis Decomposition Network for multi-frame scenarios. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our networks achieve state-of-the-art (SOTA) performance across multiple benchmarks, offering a superior balance between detection accuracy and computational efficiency. Our codes will be made public at: https://github.com/greekinRoma/IRSTD HC Platform. Published: 2026-06-08T19:51:37+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing Score: 0.813 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Chen Hu; Mingyu Zhou; Shuai Yuan; Hongbo Hu; Zhenming Peng; Tian Pu; Xiying Li

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing

DOI: 10.1109/tgrs.2026.3701189

Relevance: 0.813 (must_read)

A key challenge in infrared small target detection (IRSTD) is that weak target signal responses are easily obscured by strong background clutter, frequently resulting in missed detections. While traditional gradient-based methods attempt to capture fine details, their robustness is limited by the static fusion of multi-directional gradient features. In this paper, we rethink feature fusion from the perspective of Basis Decomposition Theory and propose a novel framework that reformulates the process into an explicit and adaptive decomposition-and-reconstruction paradigm. Specifically, we introduce the Basis Decomposition Module (BDM) and its specialized variant, the Gradient Decomposition Module (GDM) for IRSTD. GDMs treat the normalized gradient features as basis vectors to reconstruct a new feature, thereby maintaining detailed structures and highlighting infrared small targets. By integrating GDMs into a lightweight three-stage U-Net, we develop two unified architectures: the Spatial Gradient Basis Decomposition Network for single-frame detection and the Spatio-temporal Gradient Basis Decomposition Network for multi-frame scenarios. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our networks achieve state-of-the-art (SOTA) performance across multiple benchmarks, offering a superior balance between detection accuracy and computational efficiency. Our codes will be made public at: https://github.com/greekinRoma/IRSTD HC Platform.

item.title: HPHF-MMDet: Heterogeneous Perception and Hyperspherical Fusion for Multimodal Object Detection
item.dc:creator: Guohua Lv
Zhengyang Zhang
Guangxiao Ma
Yongbiao Gao
Jihao Jin
Qian Xu
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology
item.description: Multimodal object detection aims to leverage the complementary characteristics of visible (RGB) and infrared (IR) images to address perception challenges in complex environments. However, existing methods typically utilize structurally uniform backbones that neglect the intrinsic disparities in imaging mechanisms between RGB and IR modalities. Moreover, existing fusion methods fail to effectively address the feature distribution discrepancies across heterogeneous modalities. To address these issues, we propose Heterogeneous Perception and Hyperspherical Fusion for Multimodal Object Detection (HPHF-MMDet). We devise a Modality-Specific Heterogeneous Backbone (MSHB) that diverges from the conventional symmetric feature extraction paradigm. Specifically, for the RGB modality, we employ a Context-Guided Detail Enhancement Module (CGDEM) to dynamically modulate detailed features, suppressing background interference while highlighting target textures. For the IR modality, a Multi-Scale Windmill Convolution (MSWC) is carefully designed to exploit asymmetric receptive fields for capturing the morphological features of thermal targets. Furthermore, to bridge the feature distribution discrepancies between heterogeneous modalities, we introduce a Cross-Modal Complementary Hyperspherical Fusion (CM-CHF) mechanism, which aggregates complementary information through bidirectional hyperspherical alignment. We conduct extensive experiments on the LLVIP, M3FD, and MFAD datasets. Our method achieves mAP50 of 97.9%, 89.8%, and 80.8% respectively, significantly outperforming existing state-of-the-art approaches. The source code is available at https://github.com/zzyadad/HPHF-MMDet. Published: 2026-06-09T19:55:00+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology Score: 0.813 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Guohua Lv; Zhengyang Zhang; Guangxiao Ma; Yongbiao Gao; Jihao Jin; Qian Xu

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology

DOI: 10.1109/tcsvt.2026.3701598

Relevance: 0.813 (must_read)

Multimodal object detection aims to leverage the complementary characteristics of visible (RGB) and infrared (IR) images to address perception challenges in complex environments. However, existing methods typically utilize structurally uniform backbones that neglect the intrinsic disparities in imaging mechanisms between RGB and IR modalities. Moreover, existing fusion methods fail to effectively address the feature distribution discrepancies across heterogeneous modalities. To address these issues, we propose Heterogeneous Perception and Hyperspherical Fusion for Multimodal Object Detection (HPHF-MMDet). We devise a Modality-Specific Heterogeneous Backbone (MSHB) that diverges from the conventional symmetric feature extraction paradigm. Specifically, for the RGB modality, we employ a Context-Guided Detail Enhancement Module (CGDEM) to dynamically modulate detailed features, suppressing background interference while highlighting target textures. For the IR modality, a Multi-Scale Windmill Convolution (MSWC) is carefully designed to exploit asymmetric receptive fields for capturing the morphological features of thermal targets. Furthermore, to bridge the feature distribution discrepancies between heterogeneous modalities, we introduce a Cross-Modal Complementary Hyperspherical Fusion (CM-CHF) mechanism, which aggregates complementary information through bidirectional hyperspherical alignment. We conduct extensive experiments on the LLVIP, M3FD, and MFAD datasets. Our method achieves mAP50 of 97.9%, 89.8%, and 80.8% respectively, significantly outperforming existing state-of-the-art approaches. The source code is available at https://github.com/zzyadad/HPHF-MMDet.

item.title: SpecEStop: Self-Supervised Hyperspectral Mixed Noise Removal via Deep Spectral Prior
item.dc:creator: Ruobing Zhang
Michael K. Ng
Lianru Gao
Marina Ljubenovic
Lina Zhuang
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Image Processing
item.description: Hyperspectral remote sensing images often suffer from mixed noise—Gaussian, stripe, and impulse—due to atmospheric interference, solar variability, and sensor imperfections. These noises are typically band-dependent and diverse in distribution, making unified denoising particularly challenging. Existing deep denoising methods rely on clean/noisy pairs, which are unavailable in real-world remote sensing, while traditional approaches require manual tuning and lack adaptability. We propose SpecEStop, a fully self-supervised spectral vector denoising framework that requires only a single noisy HSI for training. Leveraging a novel deep spectral prior, SpecEStop exploits the spectral bias of neural networks, which tend to learn low-frequency (clean) signal components with Gaussian noise before overfitting to non-Gaussian noise ones. An adaptive early stopping strategy halts training before non-Gaussian noise is learned, enabling effective suppression of complex noise patterns. To address remaining Gaussian noise, we purposely design the network architecture to preserve its statistical properties in the latent space, allowing the use of off-the-shelf Gaussian denoisers during inference. Without any clean supervision, SpecEStop achieves effective, stage-wise removal of mixed noise, as validated across diverse real-world scenarios. Code will be released at https://github.com/ruobing-Zhang1. Published: 2026-06-08T19:54:34+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Image Processing Score: 0.808 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Ruobing Zhang; Michael K. Ng; Lianru Gao; Marina Ljubenovic; Lina Zhuang

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Image Processing

DOI: 10.1109/tip.2026.3699095

Relevance: 0.808 (must_read)

Hyperspectral remote sensing images often suffer from mixed noise—Gaussian, stripe, and impulse—due to atmospheric interference, solar variability, and sensor imperfections. These noises are typically band-dependent and diverse in distribution, making unified denoising particularly challenging. Existing deep denoising methods rely on clean/noisy pairs, which are unavailable in real-world remote sensing, while traditional approaches require manual tuning and lack adaptability. We propose SpecEStop, a fully self-supervised spectral vector denoising framework that requires only a single noisy HSI for training. Leveraging a novel deep spectral prior, SpecEStop exploits the spectral bias of neural networks, which tend to learn low-frequency (clean) signal components with Gaussian noise before overfitting to non-Gaussian noise ones. An adaptive early stopping strategy halts training before non-Gaussian noise is learned, enabling effective suppression of complex noise patterns. To address remaining Gaussian noise, we purposely design the network architecture to preserve its statistical properties in the latent space, allowing the use of off-the-shelf Gaussian denoisers during inference. Without any clean supervision, SpecEStop achieves effective, stage-wise removal of mixed noise, as validated across diverse real-world scenarios. Code will be released at https://github.com/ruobing-Zhang1.

item.title: A Brain-inspired Structurally Searchable Model for SAR Ship Detection in Complex Environments
item.dc:creator: Moran Ju
Tengkai Mao
Mulin Li
Si-nian Jin
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
item.description: Synthetic Aperture Radar (SAR) ship detection remains challenging due to complex backgrounds, arbitrary object orientations, and imaging noise, which often leads to high computational costs for CNN-based models. However, noise from SAR imaging complicates the background, increasing detection difficulty and hardware resource demands, thus limiting model applications. Recently, brain-inspired computing based on visual filtering mechanisms has witnessed rapid development. A large amount of data has also verified the feasibility of applying brain-inspired computing in various disciplines. Inspired by visual filtering mechanisms, we propose a brain-inspired, architecture searchable SAR image detection model using neural architecture search (NAS). Unlike other models, we specifically introduce a brain-inspired filtering module prior to detection to enhance input image quality and target noise suppression. Our model comprises two parts: a brain-inspired dynamic filtering task designed to enhance ship objects features while suppressing complex backgrounds, and a DETR-inspired dynamic detection task in polar coordinates. In the filtering task, we introduce the Eye Kernel for better feature extraction with object mask information and propose dynamic deformable convolution to mimic the brain's dynamic receptive field. In the detection task, we propose an Efficient Lightweight Feed-Forward Network to reduces parameters while maintaining performance. For rotating object s, we propose Rotated Multi-scale Attention to avoid sampling non-object regions, reducing model size. The model uses an improved one-to-many Hungarian algorithm in the polar coordinate system to regress the oriented bounding boxes (OBB). Compared with previous state-of-the-art models, our model has achieved remarkable enhancements and far outperforms other models. Furthermore, our baseline model achieves state-of-the-art performance on RSSDD and RSDD datasets, with mAP improvements of 0.89% and 0.42% respectivel... Published: 2026-06-08T19:52:06+00:00 Venue: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing Score: 0.808 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Moran Ju; Tengkai Mao; Mulin Li; Si-nian Jin

Journal: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing

DOI: 10.1109/jstars.2026.3701637

Relevance: 0.808 (must_read)

Synthetic Aperture Radar (SAR) ship detection remains challenging due to complex backgrounds, arbitrary object orientations, and imaging noise, which often leads to high computational costs for CNN-based models. However, noise from SAR imaging complicates the background, increasing detection difficulty and hardware resource demands, thus limiting model applications. Recently, brain-inspired computing based on visual filtering mechanisms has witnessed rapid development. A large amount of data has also verified the feasibility of applying brain-inspired computing in various disciplines. Inspired by visual filtering mechanisms, we propose a brain-inspired, architecture searchable SAR image detection model using neural architecture search (NAS). Unlike other models, we specifically introduce a brain-inspired filtering module prior to detection to enhance input image quality and target noise suppression. Our model comprises two parts: a brain-inspired dynamic filtering task designed to enhance ship objects features while suppressing complex backgrounds, and a DETR-inspired dynamic detection task in polar coordinates. In the filtering task, we introduce the Eye Kernel for better feature extraction with object mask information and propose dynamic deformable convolution to mimic the brain's dynamic receptive field. In the detection task, we propose an Efficient Lightweight Feed-Forward Network to reduces parameters while maintaining performance. For rotating object s, we propose Rotated Multi-scale Attention to avoid sampling non-object regions, reducing model size. The model uses an improved one-to-many Hungarian algorithm in the polar coordinate system to regress the oriented bounding boxes (OBB). Compared with previous state-of-the-art models, our model has achieved remarkable enhancements and far outperforms other models. Furthermore, our baseline model achieves state-of-the-art performance on RSSDD and RSDD datasets, with mAP improvements of 0.89% and 0.42% respectivel...

item.title: Few-Shot Domain Adaptation Transformer for Hyperspectral Image Change Detection
item.dc:creator: Yanheng Wang
Jianjun Sha
Kai Qin
Jianghe Xing
Yongsheng Gao
Lianru Gao
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
item.description: Deep learning has shown strong performance in hyperspectral images change detection (HSIs-CD), but it typically requires a large amount of labeled data. Acquiring such labeled data is often expensive, time-consuming, and infeasible in many application scenarios. Few-shot learning (FSL) addresses this limitation by training a model on a large number of training tasks and then applying it to test tasks with only a few labeled samples. However, in HSIs-CD, variations in imaging conditions lead to significant distribution gaps across datasets. Consequently, FSL models trained on the training tasks typically generalize poorly to test tasks and may fail to extract meaningful features. To address this, we propose FSDA-Former, a few-shot domain adaptation transformer for HSIs-CD. First, it employs a generative pretraining strategy based on a masked autoencoder (FSDA-MAE) to learn domain-agnostic representations and improve feature extraction across diverse tasks. Second, an FSDA Transformer layer (FSDA-TL) is introduced to refine the extracted features in a meta-learning manner, thereby learning class-discriminative representations and constructing a task-specific metric space. Third, a two-stage domain adaptation strategy (FSDA-TDA) is developed to further adapt FSDA-Former to the feature domain of the test task. FSDA-TDA further enhances the generalization ability of FSDA-Former under distribution shifts between training and test tasks. Experimental results on three real-world HSIs-CD datasets demonstrate that FSDA-Former consistently outperforms existing few-shot baselines. It achieves up to 3.60% improvement in Kappa and 1.58% in overall accuracy. These results demonstrate the effectiveness and superiority of FSDA-Former in HSIs-CD, even when only a few labeled examples are available. Published: 2026-06-08T19:51:37+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing Score: 0.806 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Yanheng Wang; Jianjun Sha; Kai Qin; Jianghe Xing; Yongsheng Gao; Lianru Gao

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing

DOI: 10.1109/tgrs.2026.3700816

Relevance: 0.806 (must_read)

Deep learning has shown strong performance in hyperspectral images change detection (HSIs-CD), but it typically requires a large amount of labeled data. Acquiring such labeled data is often expensive, time-consuming, and infeasible in many application scenarios. Few-shot learning (FSL) addresses this limitation by training a model on a large number of training tasks and then applying it to test tasks with only a few labeled samples. However, in HSIs-CD, variations in imaging conditions lead to significant distribution gaps across datasets. Consequently, FSL models trained on the training tasks typically generalize poorly to test tasks and may fail to extract meaningful features. To address this, we propose FSDA-Former, a few-shot domain adaptation transformer for HSIs-CD. First, it employs a generative pretraining strategy based on a masked autoencoder (FSDA-MAE) to learn domain-agnostic representations and improve feature extraction across diverse tasks. Second, an FSDA Transformer layer (FSDA-TL) is introduced to refine the extracted features in a meta-learning manner, thereby learning class-discriminative representations and constructing a task-specific metric space. Third, a two-stage domain adaptation strategy (FSDA-TDA) is developed to further adapt FSDA-Former to the feature domain of the test task. FSDA-TDA further enhances the generalization ability of FSDA-Former under distribution shifts between training and test tasks. Experimental results on three real-world HSIs-CD datasets demonstrate that FSDA-Former consistently outperforms existing few-shot baselines. It achieves up to 3.60% improvement in Kappa and 1.58% in overall accuracy. These results demonstrate the effectiveness and superiority of FSDA-Former in HSIs-CD, even when only a few labeled examples are available.

item.title: Multiscale Difference Excitation Networks for Efficient Salient Object Detection in Remote Sensing Images
item.dc:creator: Jiehua Zhang
Li Liu
Zhuo Su
Longguang Wang
Tianpeng Liu
Zhen Liu
Matti Pietik¨ainen
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
item.description: Developing lightweight architectures for Optical Remote Sensing Image Salient Object Detection (ORSI-SOD) is essential to enable real-time interpretation of ORSI scenes on resource-constrained satellite platforms. Existing lightweight ORSI-SOD methods leverage lightweight encoders and carefully designed modules to enhance efficiency while maintaining performance. However, these methods often rely on intricate multistage feature aggregation strategies to mitigate background interference and enhance feature response of salient objects, inevitably compromising the accuracy-efficiency balance. To address this issue, we propose the Multiscale Difference Excitation Network (MSDEN), a novel lightweight ORSI-SOD architecture that optimizes the accuracy-efficiency trade-off. Our approach first incorporates the Multiscale Difference Excitation Guidance Module (MDEGM), which explicitly models local boundary cues and mitigates the suppression of object features caused by redundant backgrounds. Additionally, we employ the Compact Spatial Feature Fusion Module (CSFM) to capture long-range contextual information, facilitating the refinement of salient object representations across diverse scales. We conduct extensive experiments on three public benchmark datasets, demonstrating that the proposed MSDEN substantially narrows the performance gap between lightweight models and recent state-of-the-art ORSI-SOD methods. It achieves the Sα of 0.9303 in the EORSSD dataset, with only 2.7M parameters and 1.0G FLOPs for 288×288 inputs. Published: 2026-06-08T19:51:37+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing Score: 0.806 (must_read)

Authors: Jiehua Zhang; Li Liu; Zhuo Su; Longguang Wang; Tianpeng Liu; Zhen Liu; Matti Pietik¨ainen

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing

Relevance: 0.806 (must_read)

Developing lightweight architectures for Optical Remote Sensing Image Salient Object Detection (ORSI-SOD) is essential to enable real-time interpretation of ORSI scenes on resource-constrained satellite platforms. Existing lightweight ORSI-SOD methods leverage lightweight encoders and carefully designed modules to enhance efficiency while maintaining performance. However, these methods often rely on intricate multistage feature aggregation strategies to mitigate background interference and enhance feature response of salient objects, inevitably compromising the accuracy-efficiency balance. To address this issue, we propose the Multiscale Difference Excitation Network (MSDEN), a novel lightweight ORSI-SOD architecture that optimizes the accuracy-efficiency trade-off. Our approach first incorporates the Multiscale Difference Excitation Guidance Module (MDEGM), which explicitly models local boundary cues and mitigates the suppression of object features caused by redundant backgrounds. Additionally, we employ the Compact Spatial Feature Fusion Module (CSFM) to capture long-range contextual information, facilitating the refinement of salient object representations across diverse scales. We conduct extensive experiments on three public benchmark datasets, demonstrating that the proposed MSDEN substantially narrows the performance gap between lightweight models and recent state-of-the-art ORSI-SOD methods. It achieves the Sα of 0.9303 in the EORSSD dataset, with only 2.7M parameters and 1.0G FLOPs for 288×288 inputs.

Machine learning methods for drug discovery often face difficulties in identifying novel bioactive molecules that do not belong to the training data distribution. A new metric can now quantify chemical distribution shift and evaluate the generalization capability of molecular machine learning models.

item.title: Reinforcing Out-of-Distribution SAR Ship Detection: A Guidance-Constraint Hybrid Approach
item.dc:creator: Qiansheng Ma
Zhe Chen
Yun Zhang
Zhiquan Ding
Qinglong Hua
Zhenxi Zhang
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
item.description: Out-of-distribution (OOD) generalization remains a critical challenge in Synthetic Aperture Radar (SAR) ship detection, where training and test data often originate from different distributions. Conventional approaches such as Domain Generalization (DG) and Domain Adaptation (DA) rely heavily on data diversity, limiting their efficacy in real-world scenarios with limited training data. To address this, we propose a guidance-constraint hybrid framework that integrates structured knowledge into data-driven detection pipeline. Candidate regions are first identified using a classical local Constant False Alarm Rate (CFAR) detector, serving as guidance. Confidence estimation is then refined via {2^{{\mathrm{{nd}}}}} {2^{{\mathrm{{nd}}}}} order Gaussian filtration, acting as a constraint to suppress noise and enhance spatial coherence. The data-driven branch employs Enhanced ShuffleNet v2 (ESv2), a lightweight architecture designed to improve semantic correlation and attention to relevant regions. Experimental results demonstrate that our hybrid model achieves robust performance under severe distributional shifts, with an absolute improvement of approximately 20% in mAP@0.5 on OOD scenarios compared to conventional data-driven methods. These findings highlight the potential of informed machine learning to complement statistical approaches and advance strict OOD generalization in SAR ship detection. Published: 2026-06-09T19:52:54+00:00 Venue: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing Score: 0.796 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Qiansheng Ma; Zhe Chen; Yun Zhang; Zhiquan Ding; Qinglong Hua; Zhenxi Zhang

Journal: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing

DOI: 10.1109/jstars.2026.3701929

Relevance: 0.796 (must_read)

Out-of-distribution (OOD) generalization remains a critical challenge in Synthetic Aperture Radar (SAR) ship detection, where training and test data often originate from different distributions. Conventional approaches such as Domain Generalization (DG) and Domain Adaptation (DA) rely heavily on data diversity, limiting their efficacy in real-world scenarios with limited training data. To address this, we propose a guidance-constraint hybrid framework that integrates structured knowledge into data-driven detection pipeline. Candidate regions are first identified using a classical local Constant False Alarm Rate (CFAR) detector, serving as guidance. Confidence estimation is then refined via {2^{{\mathrm{{nd}}}}} {2^{{\mathrm{{nd}}}}} order Gaussian filtration, acting as a constraint to suppress noise and enhance spatial coherence. The data-driven branch employs Enhanced ShuffleNet v2 (ESv2), a lightweight architecture designed to improve semantic correlation and attention to relevant regions. Experimental results demonstrate that our hybrid model achieves robust performance under severe distributional shifts, with an absolute improvement of approximately 20% in mAP@0.5 on OOD scenarios compared to conventional data-driven methods. These findings highlight the potential of informed machine learning to complement statistical approaches and advance strict OOD generalization in SAR ship detection.

item.title: VQFusion: Infrared and Visible Image Fusion via Codebook Prior Guidance
item.dc:creator: Cong Bi
Xue Wang
Wenhua Qian
Kaixiang Yan
item.prism:publicationName: Information Fusion
item.description: Current infrared and visible image fusion methods often struggle to handle degradations such as noise, low contrast, blur, and adverse weather conditions, leading to the loss of fine texture details and insufficient target saliency. To address these issues, we propose VQFusion, a codebook prior-guided infrared and visible image fusion method for degraded scenarios. The proposed method consists of two stages. In the first stage, we train a vector-quantized generative adversarial network (VQGAN) to learn a semantically rich codebook prior. In the second stage, we design a feature alignment mechanism to align degraded latent features with the learned codebook distribution, thereby enabling robust quantization. To mitigate information loss during feature alignment, we further design a detail compensation module to integrate original features with quantized representations, effectively preserving fine-grained structural details. Furthermore, a bidirectional cross-attention mechanism is designed to facilitate deep cross-modal feature interaction and enhance complementary information extraction. Extensive experiments across various degradation scenarios demonstrate that VQFusion outperforms nine state-of-the-art methods in both visual quality and quantitative metrics. On the LLVIP dataset, VQFusion achieves mutual information (MI) and visual information fidelity (VIF) scores of 2.470 and 1.130, respectively, representing improvements of 20.3% and 29.0% over URFusion. Published: 2026-06-09T16:41:07+00:00 Venue: Information Fusion Score: 0.796 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Cong Bi; Xue Wang; Wenhua Qian; Kaixiang Yan

Journal: Information Fusion

DOI: 10.1016/j.inffus.2026.104544

Relevance: 0.796 (must_read)

Current infrared and visible image fusion methods often struggle to handle degradations such as noise, low contrast, blur, and adverse weather conditions, leading to the loss of fine texture details and insufficient target saliency. To address these issues, we propose VQFusion, a codebook prior-guided infrared and visible image fusion method for degraded scenarios. The proposed method consists of two stages. In the first stage, we train a vector-quantized generative adversarial network (VQGAN) to learn a semantically rich codebook prior. In the second stage, we design a feature alignment mechanism to align degraded latent features with the learned codebook distribution, thereby enabling robust quantization. To mitigate information loss during feature alignment, we further design a detail compensation module to integrate original features with quantized representations, effectively preserving fine-grained structural details. Furthermore, a bidirectional cross-attention mechanism is designed to facilitate deep cross-modal feature interaction and enhance complementary information extraction. Extensive experiments across various degradation scenarios demonstrate that VQFusion outperforms nine state-of-the-art methods in both visual quality and quantitative metrics. On the LLVIP dataset, VQFusion achieves mutual information (MI) and visual information fidelity (VIF) scores of 2.470 and 1.130, respectively, representing improvements of 20.3% and 29.0% over URFusion.

item.title: Diffusion-based Data Augmentation and Knowledge Distillation with Generated Soft Labels for Oil Spill Segmentation Learning on Satellite SAR Images
item.dc:creator: Jaeho Moon
Jeonghwan Yun
Jaehyun Kim
Jaehyup Lee
Munchurl Kim
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology
item.description: Semantic segmentation often suffers from a compounded scarcity of data, where both input observations and ground-truth labels are limited. This problem is particularly severe in remote sensing tasks such as oil spill segmentation using Synthetic Aperture Radar (SAR), where rare event occurrence and the difficulty of SAR image acquisition result in limited training data. To address this limitation, we propose a diffusion-based Data Augmentation with Knowledge Transfer (DAKTer) strategy. Our DAKTer strategy enables a diffusion model to generate SAR oil spill images along with soft label pairs, which offer richer class probability distributions than segmentation masks (i.e. hard labels). Also, for reliable joint generation of high-quality SAR images and soft labels with high correspondence, we introduce an SNR-based balancing factor aligning the noise corruption process of both modalities in diffusion models. By leveraging the generated SAR images and soft labels, a student segmentation model can learn robust feature representations without teacher models trained for the same segmentation task, improving its ability to segment oil spill regions. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our DAKTer strategy effectively transfers the knowledge of per-pixel class probabilities to the student segmentation model to distinguish the oil spill regions from other look-alike regions in the SAR images. Our DAKTer strategy boosts various segmentation models to achieve superior performance with large margins compared to other generative data augmentation methods. Published: 2026-06-09T19:55:00+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology Score: 0.795 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Jaeho Moon; Jeonghwan Yun; Jaehyun Kim; Jaehyup Lee; Munchurl Kim

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology

DOI: 10.1109/tcsvt.2026.3701691

Relevance: 0.795 (must_read)

Semantic segmentation often suffers from a compounded scarcity of data, where both input observations and ground-truth labels are limited. This problem is particularly severe in remote sensing tasks such as oil spill segmentation using Synthetic Aperture Radar (SAR), where rare event occurrence and the difficulty of SAR image acquisition result in limited training data. To address this limitation, we propose a diffusion-based Data Augmentation with Knowledge Transfer (DAKTer) strategy. Our DAKTer strategy enables a diffusion model to generate SAR oil spill images along with soft label pairs, which offer richer class probability distributions than segmentation masks (i.e. hard labels). Also, for reliable joint generation of high-quality SAR images and soft labels with high correspondence, we introduce an SNR-based balancing factor aligning the noise corruption process of both modalities in diffusion models. By leveraging the generated SAR images and soft labels, a student segmentation model can learn robust feature representations without teacher models trained for the same segmentation task, improving its ability to segment oil spill regions. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our DAKTer strategy effectively transfers the knowledge of per-pixel class probabilities to the student segmentation model to distinguish the oil spill regions from other look-alike regions in the SAR images. Our DAKTer strategy boosts various segmentation models to achieve superior performance with large margins compared to other generative data augmentation methods.

item.title: Earth-OneVision: Extending Remote Sensing Multimodal Large Language Models to More Sensor Modalities and Tasks
item.dc:creator: Miaoxin Cai
Guanqun Wang
Wei Zhang
Guangyao Zhou
Yin Zhuang
Tong Zhang
Hao Wang
He Chen
Jun Li
item.prism:publicationName: arXiv
item.description: RS-MLLMs enable natural-language understanding and spatial reasoning over earth observation imagery. However, existing models support only a narrow range of sensor types and tasks, yielding a fragmented view of the earth and leaving cross-modal geoscientific knowledge largely unexploited. This work presents Earth-OneVision, a 2B RS-MLLM that unifies six sensor modalities (i.e., optical, SAR, infrared, multispectral, temporal, and video) and cross-sensor fusion across 9 task categories within a single autoregressive framework. Three dedicated mechanisms address three bottlenecks. Full-Granularity Vision-Language Alignment (FGVLA) aligns multi-level visual features with the multi-dimensional language space. Spatial-Linguistic Isomorphic Serialization (SLIS) unifies heterogeneous spatial outputs as autoregressive tokens. Progressive Cross-Modality Adaptation (PCMA) decomposes the compound domain gap into sequential stages, tackling the viewpoint and imaging physics gaps in turn. To support joint training, MMRS-OneVision is constructed with ~34M QA pairs spanning all six sensor modalities and cross-sensor fusion across 9 task categories, substantially exceeding existing RS multimodal instruction datasets. With only 2B parameters, Earth-OneVision achieves competitive or state-of-the-art results across extensive benchmarks, consistently matching or outperforming 4B-72B RS-MLLMs. It achieves 87.52% P@0.5 on the OPT-RSVG testset for optical visual grounding and 80.68% on the SAR VQA benchmark SARLANG-Bench, exceeding 7B models by over 7%. It further achieves 75.74% recall on the BigEarthNet-MS testset for multispectral classification, and 81.94% MCQ accuracy on EarthMind-Bench for cross-modality reasoning. Published: 2026-06-09T13:01:51+00:00 Venue: arXiv Score: 0.794 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Miaoxin Cai; Guanqun Wang; Wei Zhang; Guangyao Zhou; Yin Zhuang; Tong Zhang; Hao Wang; He Chen; Jun Li

Journal: arXiv

Relevance: 0.794 (must_read)

RS-MLLMs enable natural-language understanding and spatial reasoning over earth observation imagery. However, existing models support only a narrow range of sensor types and tasks, yielding a fragmented view of the earth and leaving cross-modal geoscientific knowledge largely unexploited. This work presents Earth-OneVision, a 2B RS-MLLM that unifies six sensor modalities (i.e., optical, SAR, infrared, multispectral, temporal, and video) and cross-sensor fusion across 9 task categories within a single autoregressive framework. Three dedicated mechanisms address three bottlenecks. Full-Granularity Vision-Language Alignment (FGVLA) aligns multi-level visual features with the multi-dimensional language space. Spatial-Linguistic Isomorphic Serialization (SLIS) unifies heterogeneous spatial outputs as autoregressive tokens. Progressive Cross-Modality Adaptation (PCMA) decomposes the compound domain gap into sequential stages, tackling the viewpoint and imaging physics gaps in turn. To support joint training, MMRS-OneVision is constructed with ~34M QA pairs spanning all six sensor modalities and cross-sensor fusion across 9 task categories, substantially exceeding existing RS multimodal instruction datasets. With only 2B parameters, Earth-OneVision achieves competitive or state-of-the-art results across extensive benchmarks, consistently matching or outperforming 4B-72B RS-MLLMs. It achieves 87.52% P@0.5 on the OPT-RSVG testset for optical visual grounding and 80.68% on the SAR VQA benchmark SARLANG-Bench, exceeding 7B models by over 7%. It further achieves 75.74% recall on the BigEarthNet-MS testset for multispectral classification, and 81.94% MCQ accuracy on EarthMind-Bench for cross-modality reasoning.

item.title: DIAI-net: An Integrated Network for Deep SAR Imaging and Autofocusing
item.dc:creator: Wei Pu
Yue Song
Chao Zhou
Xiaoting Wang
Junjie Wu
Yulin Huang
Haiguang Yang
Jianyu Yang
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Aerospace and Electronic Systems
item.description: With the increasing demand for high-precision imaging, synthetic aperture radar (SAR) has seen increasingly widespread applications in the field of remote sensing, and deep learning SAR imaging net has achieved fruitful developments. However, due to deviations in the trajectory, motion errors are present in the actual SAR echoes. Therefore, developing an integrated network for imaging and auto-focus to obtain high-precision SAR images holds meaningful importance. To address this issue, this paper proposes an integrated SAR imaging and autofocus strategy, i.e., (DIAI-net). The proposed DIAI-net is composed of two sub-networks, i.e., Image Reconstruction based on deep SAR imaging prior network (IrDIP) and Measurement matrix Estimation based on deep SAR imaging prior network (MeDIP). In both IrDIP and MeDIP, unrolled Alternating Direction Method of Multipliers (ADMM) network architecture is applied to exploit prior information. Testing results on both simulated and real SAR data demonstrate that the proposed DIAI-net leads to more accurate estimation of measurement matrix and better imaging results compared with the existing SAR auto-focus techniques. Published: 2026-06-09T19:55:15+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Aerospace and Electronic Systems Score: 0.794 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Wei Pu; Yue Song; Chao Zhou; Xiaoting Wang; Junjie Wu; Yulin Huang; Haiguang Yang; Jianyu Yang

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Aerospace and Electronic Systems

DOI: 10.1109/taes.2026.3701857

Relevance: 0.794 (must_read)

With the increasing demand for high-precision imaging, synthetic aperture radar (SAR) has seen increasingly widespread applications in the field of remote sensing, and deep learning SAR imaging net has achieved fruitful developments. However, due to deviations in the trajectory, motion errors are present in the actual SAR echoes. Therefore, developing an integrated network for imaging and auto-focus to obtain high-precision SAR images holds meaningful importance. To address this issue, this paper proposes an integrated SAR imaging and autofocus strategy, i.e., (DIAI-net). The proposed DIAI-net is composed of two sub-networks, i.e., Image Reconstruction based on deep SAR imaging prior network (IrDIP) and Measurement matrix Estimation based on deep SAR imaging prior network (MeDIP). In both IrDIP and MeDIP, unrolled Alternating Direction Method of Multipliers (ADMM) network architecture is applied to exploit prior information. Testing results on both simulated and real SAR data demonstrate that the proposed DIAI-net leads to more accurate estimation of measurement matrix and better imaging results compared with the existing SAR auto-focus techniques.

item.title: Stage-Wise Feature-Guided Imbalance Diffusion Model for Imbalanced Semi-Supervised SAR Target Recognition
item.dc:creator: Xinzheng Zhang
Guopeng Li
Yuqing Luo
Dong Li
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Aerospace and Electronic Systems
item.description: Automatic Target Recognition (ATR) in Synthetic Aperture Radar (SAR) imagery plays a key role in various defense and civilian applications. While recent semi-supervised learning methods have advanced SAR ATR, they often struggle when training data is class-imbalanced-a common scenario in practice. To address this challenge, we propose a method that combines class-rebalancing with self-training. Our approach consists of two main parts: a sample synthesis module and a semi-supervised learning module. The synthesis module uses a specially designed diffusion model to generate realistic samples for minority classes, guided by both global features and scattering center features in a stage-wise manner. The semi-supervised module introduces a Bayesian-rebalancing energy score to improve pseudo-label screening, reducing bias toward majority classes. Together, these components help the model learn a more balanced and accurate classifier. Experiments on highly imbalanced SAR datasets show that our method significantly improves recognition performance and reduces class imbalance effects. Published: 2026-06-09T19:55:15+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Aerospace and Electronic Systems Score: 0.793 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Xinzheng Zhang; Guopeng Li; Yuqing Luo; Dong Li

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Aerospace and Electronic Systems

DOI: 10.1109/taes.2026.3701860

Relevance: 0.793 (must_read)

Automatic Target Recognition (ATR) in Synthetic Aperture Radar (SAR) imagery plays a key role in various defense and civilian applications. While recent semi-supervised learning methods have advanced SAR ATR, they often struggle when training data is class-imbalanced-a common scenario in practice. To address this challenge, we propose a method that combines class-rebalancing with self-training. Our approach consists of two main parts: a sample synthesis module and a semi-supervised learning module. The synthesis module uses a specially designed diffusion model to generate realistic samples for minority classes, guided by both global features and scattering center features in a stage-wise manner. The semi-supervised module introduces a Bayesian-rebalancing energy score to improve pseudo-label screening, reducing bias toward majority classes. Together, these components help the model learn a more balanced and accurate classifier. Experiments on highly imbalanced SAR datasets show that our method significantly improves recognition performance and reduces class imbalance effects.

item.title: Multi-Scale Symmetric Difference Attention-Based Transformer for Panchromatic and Multispectral Classification
item.dc:creator: Yuantian Zhu
Hao Zhu
Zeyu Lei
Xiaotong Li
Huihui Dong
Biao Hou
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
item.description: Nowadays, effectively exploiting multispectral (MS) and panchromatic (PAN) imagery for their joint classification task has attracted wide attention. However, due to their heterogeneous resolutions and distinct modality characteristics, the precise alignment of different-scale semantic features and the compact fusion of modality-specific information remain challenging. To tackle these issues, we propose a multi-scale symmetric difference attention-based Transformer (MSAFormer). It comprises two key modules: texture-aware multi-scale feature generation (TMFG) module and symmetric difference feature divergence interaction (SDFD) module. By exploiting PAN’s high-resolution texture to bridge the inherent texture deficiency of MS at continuous resolutions, TMFG builds a multi-scale space with precise texture alignment. This module constructs a robust foundation for the comprehensive extraction of dual-source specific features during interaction. Subsequently, by applying the concept of symmetric difference between sets to the aligned dual-modality groups of different scales, SDFD generates compact and comprehensive multi-scale fusion representations in a non-decoupled manner. These lightweight fusion features integrate the enhanced modality-specific features and eliminate the overlapped redundancy of shared information, ultimately yielding superior classification performance. Several experimental results validate the effectiveness and robustness of the network. Codes are available at https://github.com/Xidian-AIGroup190726/MSAformer. Published: 2026-06-09T19:52:19+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing Score: 0.793 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Yuantian Zhu; Hao Zhu; Zeyu Lei; Xiaotong Li; Huihui Dong; Biao Hou

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing

DOI: 10.1109/tgrs.2026.3701412

Relevance: 0.793 (must_read)

Nowadays, effectively exploiting multispectral (MS) and panchromatic (PAN) imagery for their joint classification task has attracted wide attention. However, due to their heterogeneous resolutions and distinct modality characteristics, the precise alignment of different-scale semantic features and the compact fusion of modality-specific information remain challenging. To tackle these issues, we propose a multi-scale symmetric difference attention-based Transformer (MSAFormer). It comprises two key modules: texture-aware multi-scale feature generation (TMFG) module and symmetric difference feature divergence interaction (SDFD) module. By exploiting PAN’s high-resolution texture to bridge the inherent texture deficiency of MS at continuous resolutions, TMFG builds a multi-scale space with precise texture alignment. This module constructs a robust foundation for the comprehensive extraction of dual-source specific features during interaction. Subsequently, by applying the concept of symmetric difference between sets to the aligned dual-modality groups of different scales, SDFD generates compact and comprehensive multi-scale fusion representations in a non-decoupled manner. These lightweight fusion features integrate the enhanced modality-specific features and eliminate the overlapped redundancy of shared information, ultimately yielding superior classification performance. Several experimental results validate the effectiveness and robustness of the network. Codes are available at https://github.com/Xidian-AIGroup190726/MSAformer.

item.title: Nash Equilibrium-Guided Dynamic Cross-Scale Fusion and Manifold Alignment for Remote Sensing Image Semantic Segmentation
item.dc:creator: Yuanxu Wang
Yurong Qian
Lu Bai
Jinmiao Song
Jun Long
item.prism:publicationName: Information Fusion
item.description: Effective semantic segmentation of high-resolution remote sensing images requires robust fusion of multi-scale information. However, a fundamental conflict persists between high-level semantic abstraction and low-level spatial precision, known as scale heterogeneity. Current fusion approaches generally depend on passive aggregation strategies, lacking an active mechanism to resolve semantic discrepancies. This often results in weak signals being obscured by extensive background noise. To address this issue, we present DCIGNet, a novel framework based on a game-theoretic information fusion paradigm. First, we introduce the Dynamic Cross-Scale Bidirectional Interaction Fusion (DCSBIF) module. Unlike static weights, this module models the interaction between heterogeneous feature scales as a non-cooperative game that aims for a Nash equilibrium. This mechanism actively arbitrates the flow of information, dynamically suppressing conflicting noise and amplifying target saliency through a content-driven equilibrium process. Next, to address information loss in anisotropic and non-rigid targets, we integrate orthogonal tensor factorisation and manifold-aware sampling into the fusion pipeline. These mechanisms explicitly decouple global directional priors and align receptive fields with intrinsic object manifolds to ensure geometric fidelity during feature reconstruction. Extensive experiments on the ISPRS Potsdam, Vaihingen and LoveDA datasets demonstrate that DCIGNet significantly outperforms current state-of-the-art methods in terms of both geometric integrity and scale robustness. Specifically, the proposed game-theoretic fusion strategy strikes an optimal balance between segmentation accuracy and computational efficiency, providing a robust solution for interpreting multi-source geospatial information. Published: 2026-06-08T16:42:17+00:00 Venue: Information Fusion Score: 0.793 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Yuanxu Wang; Yurong Qian; Lu Bai; Jinmiao Song; Jun Long

Journal: Information Fusion

DOI: 10.1016/j.inffus.2026.104520

Relevance: 0.793 (must_read)

Effective semantic segmentation of high-resolution remote sensing images requires robust fusion of multi-scale information. However, a fundamental conflict persists between high-level semantic abstraction and low-level spatial precision, known as scale heterogeneity. Current fusion approaches generally depend on passive aggregation strategies, lacking an active mechanism to resolve semantic discrepancies. This often results in weak signals being obscured by extensive background noise. To address this issue, we present DCIGNet, a novel framework based on a game-theoretic information fusion paradigm. First, we introduce the Dynamic Cross-Scale Bidirectional Interaction Fusion (DCSBIF) module. Unlike static weights, this module models the interaction between heterogeneous feature scales as a non-cooperative game that aims for a Nash equilibrium. This mechanism actively arbitrates the flow of information, dynamically suppressing conflicting noise and amplifying target saliency through a content-driven equilibrium process. Next, to address information loss in anisotropic and non-rigid targets, we integrate orthogonal tensor factorisation and manifold-aware sampling into the fusion pipeline. These mechanisms explicitly decouple global directional priors and align receptive fields with intrinsic object manifolds to ensure geometric fidelity during feature reconstruction. Extensive experiments on the ISPRS Potsdam, Vaihingen and LoveDA datasets demonstrate that DCIGNet significantly outperforms current state-of-the-art methods in terms of both geometric integrity and scale robustness. Specifically, the proposed game-theoretic fusion strategy strikes an optimal balance between segmentation accuracy and computational efficiency, providing a robust solution for interpreting multi-source geospatial information.

item.title: Proposal Refinement for Few-Shot Object Detection
item.dc:creator: Yuan Zeng
Bin Song
Jie Guo
Yuwen Chen
item.prism:publicationName: arXiv
item.description: Few-shot object detection has gained widely attention in recent years. Some excellent algorithms have been proposed to handle this task. However, most of these algorithms rely on the performance of few-shot classification. Unlike previous attempts, our work focuses on the problem of unbalanced distribution of region proposals between the novel classes and the base classes. In order to alleviate this unbalanced distribution, we propose the proposal refinement approach for different training phases. Specifically, refinement loss is designed for the base training phase to enhance sensitivity of the model to novel classes, and refinement branch is introduced as an auxiliary branch for RPN (Region Proposal Networks) to generate more novel proposals in the fine-tuning phase. By rebalancing the proposal distribution, the proposed approach outperforms the baselines methods by roughly 1\%$\sim$6\% on current benchmarks without increasing any inference time. Through extensive experiments, we prove that we establish a new state-of-the-art method for the few-shot object detection task. Published: 2026-06-08T09:20:32+00:00 Venue: arXiv Score: 0.790 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Yuan Zeng; Bin Song; Jie Guo; Yuwen Chen

Journal: arXiv

Relevance: 0.790 (must_read)

Few-shot object detection has gained widely attention in recent years. Some excellent algorithms have been proposed to handle this task. However, most of these algorithms rely on the performance of few-shot classification. Unlike previous attempts, our work focuses on the problem of unbalanced distribution of region proposals between the novel classes and the base classes. In order to alleviate this unbalanced distribution, we propose the proposal refinement approach for different training phases. Specifically, refinement loss is designed for the base training phase to enhance sensitivity of the model to novel classes, and refinement branch is introduced as an auxiliary branch for RPN (Region Proposal Networks) to generate more novel proposals in the fine-tuning phase. By rebalancing the proposal distribution, the proposed approach outperforms the baselines methods by roughly 1\%$\sim$6\% on current benchmarks without increasing any inference time. Through extensive experiments, we prove that we establish a new state-of-the-art method for the few-shot object detection task.

item.title: MSAM: Multi-Semantic Adaptive Mining for Cross-Modal Drone Video-Text Retrieval
item.dc:creator: Jinghao Huang
Yaxiong Chen
Ganchao Liu
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology
item.description: With the advancement of drone technology, the volume of video data increases rapidly, creating an urgent need for efficient semantic retrieval. This work presents one of the early studies on the drone video-text retrieval (DVTR) task. Drone videos feature overhead perspectives, strong structural homogeneity, and diverse semantic expressions of target combinations, which challenge existing cross-modal methods designed for ground-level views in effectively modeling their characteristics. Therefore, dedicated retrieval mechanisms tailored for drone scenarios are necessary. To address this issue, we propose a novel approach called Multi-Semantic Adaptive Mining (MSAM). MSAM introduces a multi-semantic adaptive learning mechanism that models dynamic changes across frames and extracts region-level semantic information. This design improves fine-grained cross-modal alignment. This method relies on fine-grained interactions between textual tokens and drone video frames. It integrates an adaptive semantic construction module, a distribution-driven semantic learning term, and a diversity-aware semantic constraint to enhance cross-modal interactions and improve the robustness of feature representations. To reduce the interference of complex backgrounds in drone videos, we introduce a cross-modal interactive feature fusion pooling mechanism that focuses on feature extraction and matching in target regions, minimizing noise effects. Extensive experiments on two self-constructed drone video-text datasets show that MSAM outperforms other existing methods in the DVTR task. The code and data are accessible at https://github.com/huangjh98/MSAM. Published: 2026-06-09T19:55:00+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology Score: 0.789 (must_read)

With the advancement of drone technology, the volume of video data increases rapidly, creating an urgent need for efficient semantic retrieval. This work presents one of the early studies on the drone video-text retrieval (DVTR) task. Drone videos feature overhead perspectives, strong structural homogeneity, and diverse semantic expressions of target combinations, which challenge existing cross-modal methods designed for ground-level views in effectively modeling their characteristics. Therefore, dedicated retrieval mechanisms tailored for drone scenarios are necessary. To address this issue, we propose a novel approach called Multi-Semantic Adaptive Mining (MSAM). MSAM introduces a multi-semantic adaptive learning mechanism that models dynamic changes across frames and extracts region-level semantic information. This design improves fine-grained cross-modal alignment. This method relies on fine-grained interactions between textual tokens and drone video frames. It integrates an adaptive semantic construction module, a distribution-driven semantic learning term, and a diversity-aware semantic constraint to enhance cross-modal interactions and improve the robustness of feature representations. To reduce the interference of complex backgrounds in drone videos, we introduce a cross-modal interactive feature fusion pooling mechanism that focuses on feature extraction and matching in target regions, minimizing noise effects. Extensive experiments on two self-constructed drone video-text datasets show that MSAM outperforms other existing methods in the DVTR task. The code and data are accessible at https://github.com/huangjh98/MSAM.

item.title: FLoRA: Fusion-Latent for Optical Reconstruction and Flood Area Segmentation via Cross-Modal Multi-Task Distillation Network
item.dc:creator: Jagrati Talreja
Tewodros Syum Gebre
Leila Hashemi-Beni
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
item.description: Accurate flood-water mapping is critical for disaster management, yet current methods struggle to fully exploit the potential of spaceborne imagery. Optical data offers high interpretability but is limited by environmental conditions, whereas SAR provides reliable all-weather coverage with reduced visual interpretability. FLoRA (Fusion-Latent for Optical Reconstruction & Area Segmentation) is a cross-modal multi-task framework that jointly reconstructs high-fidelity optical imagery and segments flood-water regions from Sentinel-1 SAR by fusing the complementary strengths of optical and SAR data. During training, a lightweight optical teacher (driven by RGB and NDVI priors) provides pyramidal features that guide SAR representations into a fusion-latent space via multi-scale windowed cross-attention and FiLM conditioning, with gated residuals preventing over-correction. This design enables multi-task learning across two complementary objectives: (i) SAR-to-optical translation for fine-grained RGB reconstruction and (ii) flood-water region segmentation for hydrologic interpretation. The dual decoders are optimized using Charbonnier + SSIM for structural fidelity, edge + FFT-magnitude losses for spectral realism, and Dice + BCE + hydrology-aware edge alignment for precise flood-water delineation. A feature-distillation constraint further aligns fused SAR features with the optical teacher’s manifold. Evaluations on SEN1FLOODS11, DEEPFLOOD, and SEN12MS demonstrate that FLoRA surpasses fusion baselines in PSNR, SSIM, and LPIPS, demonstrating that multi-modal fusion within a teacher-guided latent space yields semantically faithful and physically consistent flood-water intelligence from spaceborne observations. https://github.com/JagratiTalreja01/FLoRA. Published: 2026-06-08T19:51:37+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing Score: 0.788 (must_read)
